title: caladaptaer
text: caladaptaer reads downscaled CMIP6 climate data from California’s Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine directly into R. No Python, no API key, no authentication – just GDAL reading Zarr stores from AWS S3.

title: caladaptaer
text:
Available Data











Dataset
Resolution
Temporal
GCMs
SSPs
Period




WRF CMIP6 (WUS-D3)
45 / 9 / 3 km
Hourly
8 models
SSP3-7.0 (all); SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5 (CESM2)
1980–2100


LOCA2-Hybrid
3 km
Daily
15 models
SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, SSP5-8.5
1950–2100

title: caladaptaer
text:
Quick Start
library(caladaptaer)

# What's available?
cae_models(activity = "WRF")
cae_variables(activity = "WRF", timescale = "1hr")

# Read hourly temperature, 45 km grid, one day
t2 <- cae_fetch("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
               n_timesteps = 24)

# Extract time series at a point
ts <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                     lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                     n_timesteps = 24)

Installation
remotes::install_github("lebauerapproach/caladaptaer")

title: caladaptaer
text:
License
BSD 3-Clause

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
What you will learn




How to prepare CF-standard met driver files for hundreds of sites in a single pass using cae_build_met_drivers()
Why the bulk approach is dramatically more efficient than looping over cae_fetch_point()
How to set up a sites table and configure the output for use with PEcAn or standalone ecosystem model runs

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Background
Ecosystem modeling projects often need met drivers at many sites – the CCMMF project, for example, runs SIPNET at 198 design points across California. Preparing met files one site at a time with cae_fetch_point() is straightforward but wasteful: each call reads the entire WRF grid from S3 independently, so 198 sites x 8 variables x 20 years means 31,680 S3 requests.
cae_build_met_drivers() solves this by calling cae_fetch_points() (plural) under the hood. The full WRF grid is read from S3 once per variable per year, and all sites are extracted from that grid in a single vectorized operation. The same 198-site, 20-year job now takes 160 S3 requests.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Preparing the sites table
The input is a data.frame with three required columns: site_id, lon, and lat. Any additional columns are ignored.

# read CCMMF design points
sites <- read.csv("design_points.csv")
head(sites)
#>   UniqueID          site_id      lat       lon          pft
#> 1  1301797 8773c58306e8d9a0 32.71585 -115.4716 annual crop
#> 2  1300446 f2ce12d75f3b2cc6 32.75557 -115.4159 annual crop
#> ...

# cae_build_met_drivers needs site_id, lon, lat
# the site_id column is already present in this table

If your sites come from PEcAn’s BETY database or another source, just make sure you have those three columns:

# manual setup for a few sites
sites <- data.frame(
  site_id = c("fresno", "la", "sacramento"),
  lon = c(-119.77, -118.24, -121.49),
  lat = c(36.75, 34.05, 38.58)
)

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Building the drivers

library(caladaptaer)

result <- cae_build_met_drivers(
  sites     = sites,
  model     = "CESM2",
  scenario  = "ssp370",
  start_year = 2050,
  end_year   = 2055,
  outdir    = "met_drivers"
)

Progress output looks like:
Year 2050 (1 of 6)
  fetching t2
Reading grid (1 S3 fetch for all 3 sites)
Extracting 3 sites
  fetching psfc
  ...
  wrote met_drivers/fresno/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
  wrote met_drivers/la/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
  wrote met_drivers/sacramento/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
Year 2051 (2 of 6)
  ...
The result is a data.frame tracking what was written:

head(result)
#>    site_id year                                        file
#> 1   fresno 2050   met_drivers/fresno/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
#> 2       la 2050       met_drivers/la/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc
#> 3 sacramento 2050 met_drivers/sacramento/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Output file structure
Each NetCDF file contains 9 CF-standard variables at hourly resolution for one site and one year:



CF variable
Units
Source




air_temperature
K
t2 (direct)


air_pressure
Pa
psfc (direct)


precipitation_flux
kg m-2 s-1
prec / 3600


specific_humidity
1
q2 / (1 + q2)


surface_downwelling_shortwave_flux_in_air
W m-2
swdnb (direct)


surface_downwelling_longwave_flux_in_air
W m-2
lwdnb (direct)


eastward_wind
m s-1
u10 (direct)


northward_wind
m s-1
v10 (direct)


wind_speed
m s-1
sqrt(u10^2 + v10^2)



Dimensions are latitude(1), longitude(1), and time(unlimited). The time coordinate uses seconds since {year}-01-01 00:00:00.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Skipping existing files
By default, cae_build_met_drivers() skips sites that already have output files for a given year. If all sites in a year are complete, the S3 fetch for that year is skipped entirely. Pass overwrite = TRUE to regenerate everything.

# re-run after interruption -- only missing files are written
result <- cae_build_met_drivers(
  sites, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
  start_year = 2050, end_year = 2055,
  outdir = "met_drivers"
)

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Verifying the output
Spot-check a file to make sure values are physically reasonable:

library(ncdf4)

nc <- nc_open("met_drivers/fresno/CESM2.ssp370.2050.nc")
t2 <- ncvar_get(nc, "air_temperature")
pr <- ncvar_get(nc, "precipitation_flux")
nc_close(nc)

# temperature: 200--350 K
range(t2)

# precip flux: 0--0.01 kg/m2/s (0--36 mm/hr, reasonable max for hourly)
range(pr)

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Efficiency comparison
For 198 sites x 8 variables x 20 years:








Approach
S3 requests
Description




cae_fetch_point() loop
31,680
one grid read per site per var per year


cae_build_met_drivers()
160
one grid read per var per year


PEcAn download.CalAdaptWRF
160
same count, uses tempdir() cache



All three produce identical output. The choice depends on whether you are working inside PEcAn (download.CalAdaptWRF) or outside it (cae_build_met_drivers).

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
Multiple models and scenarios
To build drivers for multiple GCMs or scenarios, loop over the combinations:

models <- c("CESM2", "CNRM-ESM2-1", "EC-Earth3-Veg")

for (m in models) {
  cae_build_met_drivers(
    sites, model = m, scenario = "ssp370",
    start_year = 2050, end_year = 2069,
    outdir = file.path("met_drivers", m)
  )
}

CESM2 supports SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 in addition to SSP3-7.0. All other GCMs have SSP3-7.0 only.

title: Bulk Met Driver Prep for Multi-Site Workflows
text:
References

Rahimi, S. et al. (2024). An overview of the Western United States Dynamically Downscaled Dataset (WUS-D3). Geoscientific Model Development, 17, 2265–2286. doi:10.5194/gmd-17-2265-2024
Dietze, M. C. et al. (2014). A quantitative assessment of a terrestrial biosphere model’s data needs across North American biomes.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
What you will learn




What climate data is available on the Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine and how the CADCAT catalog organizes it
How to query the catalog to find datasets by model, scenario, variable, and resolution
How to read gridded Zarr data from AWS S3 directly into R as a stars object
How to produce California climate maps comparable to the official Python notebooks
How to extract and visualize time series at specific locations

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
The Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine
The Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine hosts roughly 1 PB of downscaled CMIP6 climate projections for California and the western US, produced for the state’s Fifth Climate Change Assessment. The data lives in cloud-optimized Zarr stores on AWS S3, publicly accessible with no credentials required.
Two downscaling products are available:
WRF CMIP6 (WUS-D3) – dynamically downscaled by UCLA’s Center for Climate Science (Rahimi et al. 2024). The Weather Research and Forecasting model produces hourly output at three nested resolutions: 45 km (d01, western US), 9 km (d02, California + surroundings), and 3 km (d03, California). Eight GCMs are downscaled, primarily under SSP3-7.0, with CESM2 also available for SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5. The WRF output includes the full surface meteorology – temperature, pressure, precipitation, humidity, radiation, and wind components.
LOCA2-Hybrid – statistically downscaled by Scripps/UCSD (Pierce et al.). Daily output at 3 km for 15 GCMs across three SSPs (SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, SSP5-8.5), covering 1950–2100. LOCA2-Hybrid uses a hybrid analog approach that substitutes WRF-derived weather patterns for end-of-century conditions where the historical analog library runs thin.
The official Python interface is climakitae. caladaptaer provides the same data access natively in R, reading Zarr stores via GDAL’s /vsis3/ virtual filesystem. No Python, no API key, no authentication.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Setup

library(caladaptaer)
library(stars)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(maps)

Check that GDAL supports Zarr (version 3.4+):

sf::sf_extSoftVersion()["GDAL"]

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Exploring the catalog
The CADCAT catalog is a CSV index of every Zarr store on S3. Each row is one variable from one model/scenario/resolution combination. cae_catalog() downloads the table once per session and caches it.

cat <- cae_catalog()
nrow(cat)
names(cat)

The catalog follows CMIP6 naming conventions:








Column
Description
Examples




activity_id
Downscaling method
WRF, LOCA2


source_id
Parent GCM
CESM2, MIROC6, EC-Earth3


experiment_id
Scenario
ssp370, ssp245, historical


table_id
Temporal resolution
1hr, day, mon


variable_id
Variable short name
t2, prec, pr, tasmax


grid_label
Spatial resolution
d01 (45 km), d02 (9 km), d03 (3 km)


member_id
Ensemble member
r1i1p1f1


path
S3 Zarr store path
s3://cadcat/wrf/ucla/cesm2/…




Data availability
The WRF and LOCA2 datasets differ in their model/scenario coverage. Use the convenience functions to see what’s available:

# WRF GCMs -- 8 models dynamically downscaled
cae_models(activity = "WRF")

# LOCA2 GCMs -- 15 models statistically downscaled
cae_models(activity = "LOCA2")

# CESM2 has the broadest scenario coverage in WRF
cae_scenarios(activity = "WRF", model = "CESM2")

# hourly WRF variables
cae_variables(activity = "WRF", timescale = "1hr")

Here’s the full picture of what’s on S3 for the WRF hourly data at 45 km:



Figure 1: WRF data availability across GCMs and scenarios. CESM2 has the broadest coverage with three SSPs; all other models have SSP3-7.0 and historical.








WRF vs LOCA2 – when to use which



Use WRF when you need hourly data, process-based meteorology (radiation, humidity, pressure), or when you care about mesoscale features like orographic precipitation or sea breeze effects. Use LOCA2 when you need more GCMs, more SSPs, or daily temperature/precipitation extremes at the finest available resolution (3 km).




Searching the catalog
cae_search() filters by any combination of fields. All filters are ANDed.

# all hourly CESM2 temperature at 45 km
cae_search(activity = "WRF", model = "CESM2",
          variable = "t2", timescale = "1hr", resolution = "d01")

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Fetching gridded data
cae_fetch() reads a Zarr store from S3 and returns a stars object. Start with n_timesteps = 1 to keep the initial read small – a single 45 km timestep is about 0.3 MB.

t2 <- cae_fetch("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
               n_timesteps = 1)
t2

The result has three dimensions: x and y in the WRF Lambert Conformal Conic projection, and time in UTC. The values are 2-meter air temperature in Kelvin.

Mapping temperature over the western US
The 45 km WRF domain covers most of western North America. Overlay state boundaries to orient the map – this is the R equivalent of what the Cal-Adapt Python notebooks do with cartopy.

# state boundaries for overlay
ca_sf <- st_as_sf(map("state", "california", plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE))
western_sf <- st_as_sf(
  map("state", c("california", "oregon", "washington", "nevada",
                  "arizona", "utah", "idaho", "montana", "wyoming",
                  "colorado", "new mexico"), plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE)
)

# transform boundaries to the WRF projection
ca_wrf <- st_transform(ca_sf, st_crs(t2))
ws_wrf <- st_transform(western_sf, st_crs(t2))

# convert to Celsius and plot
t2_c <- t2
t2_c[[1]] <- units::drop_units(t2[[1]]) - 273.15

plot(t2_c, main = "", axes = TRUE, reset = FALSE,
     col = hcl.colors(100, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE),
     key.pos = 4, key.width = lcm(1.5), key.length = 0.8)
plot(st_geometry(ws_wrf), add = TRUE, border = "grey30", lwd = 0.6)
plot(st_geometry(ca_wrf), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 1.5)
title("2-meter Air Temperature (C)\nCESM2 SSP3-7.0, WRF 45 km")




Figure 2: WRF 2-meter air temperature over the western US at 45 km resolution. The spatial pattern shows expected features – cool mountain temperatures in the Sierra Nevada and Cascades, warm desert conditions in the Southwest, and a maritime gradient along the Pacific coast. California boundary shown in black.




Shortwave radiation
Surface downwelling shortwave radiation drives photosynthesis in ecosystem models and solar generation in energy applications. The WRF field captures the diurnal solar cycle and cloud effects:

sw <- cae_fetch("swdnb", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
               n_timesteps = 1)




Figure 3: Shortwave radiation at the surface. The day-night terminator is visible as a sharp gradient. Desert regions receive the highest clear-sky irradiance. Cloud shadows appear as dark patches in otherwise sunny areas.




Surface pressure
Surface pressure is a required input for ecosystem models and reveals the topographic structure of the domain:

psfc <- cae_fetch("psfc", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                 n_timesteps = 1)




Figure 4: Surface pressure (hPa) over the western US. The topographic imprint is immediately visible – low pressure over the Rocky Mountains, Sierra Nevada, and Cascades where elevation reduces the overlying atmospheric column, and higher pressure over the Central Valley and coastal lowlands. This is one of the 7 meteorological variables SIPNET needs for carbon flux simulations.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Point extraction
For single-site analysis, cae_fetch_point() reads the grid and extracts the time series at the nearest grid cell to a lon/lat coordinate. CRS transformation from WGS84 to the WRF Lambert grid is handled automatically.

# 24 hours of temperature at Fresno, CA
ts <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                     lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                     n_timesteps = 24)
head(ts)


ts$temp_c <- ts$value - 273.15
plot(ts$time, ts$temp_c, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#D62728",
     xlab = "Time (UTC)", ylab = "Temperature (C)",
     main = "Diurnal Temperature -- Fresno, CA")
points(ts$time, ts$temp_c, pch = 16, cex = 0.6, col = "#D62728")
grid(col = "grey85")




Figure 5: Diurnal temperature cycle at Fresno extracted from the CESM2 SSP3-7.0 WRF simulation. The Central Valley in September shows cool overnight lows near 12 C and afternoon highs above 30 C – a reasonable diurnal range for the location and season.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Multi-model comparison
One of the strengths of the Cal-Adapt dataset is that multiple GCMs are available under the same downscaling framework. Comparing models at the same location reveals inter-model spread – a measure of structural uncertainty in the projections.

# get all WRF models with SSP3-7.0 (excluding ERA5 reanalysis)
wrf_models <- setdiff(cae_models(activity = "WRF"), "ERA5")
models_370 <- character(0)
for (m in wrf_models) {
  if ("ssp370" %in% cae_scenarios(activity = "WRF", model = m))
    models_370 <- c(models_370, m)
}

# extract 24-hour temperature at Fresno from each model
model_ts <- lapply(models_370, function(m) {
  d <- cae_fetch_point("t2", model = m, scenario = "ssp370",
                      lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75, n_timesteps = 24)
  d$model <- m
  d$temp_c <- d$value - 273.15
  d
})
model_ts <- do.call(rbind, model_ts)




Figure 6: Diurnal temperature from all WRF GCMs at Fresno under SSP3-7.0. The models agree on the general shape – cool night, warm afternoon – but differ by 2–5 C in the absolute magnitude. This spread reflects structural differences in how each GCM represents the large-scale circulation that drives the WRF boundary conditions.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Multiple meteorological variables
Ecosystem models and energy system models typically need several meteorological inputs. The WRF hourly dataset includes the full surface meteorology. Here we extract all 7 SIPNET-required variables at a point:



WRF name
Description
Units




t2
2-meter air temperature
K


psfc
Surface pressure
Pa


prec
Accumulated precipitation
mm/timestep


q2
Water vapor mixing ratio
kg/kg


swdnb
Downward shortwave radiation
W/m2


u10
10-meter eastward wind
m/s


v10
10-meter northward wind
m/s




vars <- c("t2", "swdnb", "prec", "q2", "psfc", "u10", "v10")
pts <- lapply(vars, function(v) {
  cae_fetch_point(v, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                 lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75, n_timesteps = 24)
})
names(pts) <- vars




Figure 7: Six-panel display of hourly meteorological variables at Fresno, CA from the CESM2 SSP3-7.0 WRF simulation. Top row: air temperature (C) and surface shortwave radiation (W/m2). Middle row: precipitation flux (kg/m2/s, converted from mm/timestep) and specific humidity (g/kg, converted from mixing ratio). Bottom row: wind speed (m/s, computed from U and V components) and surface pressure (hPa). The diurnal signatures are physically consistent – peak radiation drives peak temperature with a lag, humidity drops as temperature rises, and pressure follows a semidiurnal tide.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
What’s next

Writing CF-standard NetCDF: See the SIPNET Met Drivers vignette for the full pipeline from raw WRF variables to CF-compliant met files.
LOCA2 daily data: The same cae_fetch() interface works for LOCA2 – just specify timescale = "day" and a LOCA2 model name (e.g. "EC-Earth3").
Spatial subsetting: For California-only analysis at 9 or 3 km, use resolution = "d02" or "d03", but watch memory – a single 3 km timestep is ~150 MB.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
Requirements

R >= 4.1 (native pipe support)
GDAL >= 3.4 (Zarr driver). Check with sf::sf_extSoftVersion().
Internet access to AWS S3 (us-west-2 region). No authentication.
Memory: The 45 km grid is 199 x 199. One timestep is ~0.3 MB. A full year for one variable is ~2.6 GB.

title: Getting Started with caladaptaer
text:
References

Rahimi, S. et al. (2024). An overview of the Western United States Dynamically Downscaled Dataset (WUS-D3). Geoscientific Model Development, 17, 2265–2286. doi:10.5194/gmd-17-2265-2024
Pierce, D. W. et al. Statistical downscaling using Localized Constructed Analogs (LOCA). Journal of Hydrometeorology, 15(6), 2558–2585.
Krantz, W. et al. (2022). GCM skill evaluation for the Fifth California Climate Assessment.
Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine: https://analytics.cal-adapt.org/
CADCAT on AWS: https://registry.opendata.aws/caladapt-coproduced-climate-data/

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
What you will learn




Which meteorological variables SIPNET requires and where they come from in the WRF dataset
How WRF native units map to CF standard names and units
How to download all 7 variables and write a CF-compliant NetCDF file

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Background
SIPNET is a process-based ecosystem model that simulates carbon, water, and energy fluxes at hourly timesteps. It expects met drivers in CF-standard NetCDF format.
The Cal-Adapt WRF hourly output is a natural fit for SIPNET met forcing under future climate scenarios. This vignette walks through the pipeline: query the catalog, read all variables, convert units, and write a NetCDF.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Variable mapping
SIPNET needs 5 meteorological inputs plus wind components. The full CF-standard set also includes longwave radiation for other ecosystem models:










CF standard name
WRF name
WRF units
CF units
Conversion




air_temperature
t2
K
K
none


air_pressure
psfc
Pa
Pa
none


precipitation_flux
prec
mm/timestep
kg/m2/s
divide by dt


specific_humidity
q2
kg/kg (mixing ratio)
kg/kg (specific)
q = w/(1+w)


surface_downwelling_shortwave_flux_in_air
swdnb
W/m2
W/m2
none


surface_downwelling_longwave_flux_in_air
lwdnb
W/m2
W/m2
none


eastward_wind
u10
m/s
m/s
none


northward_wind
v10
m/s
m/s
none



Most need no conversion. Precipitation goes from accumulated mm per timestep to an instantaneous flux rate (dividing by 3600 for hourly data). Humidity goes from mixing ratio to specific humidity – the difference is small but worth getting right.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Download all variables

library(caladaptaer)

sipnet_vars <- c("t2", "psfc", "prec", "q2", "swdnb", "lwdnb", "u10", "v10")

var_data <- lapply(sipnet_vars, function(v) {
  message("reading ", v)
  cae_fetch(v, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370", n_timesteps = 24)
})
names(var_data) <- sipnet_vars

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Inspect the data
Always spot-check that values fall in physically reasonable ranges before feeding them to an ecosystem model.

# temperature: 200--350 K
summary(var_data$t2[[1]])

# pressure: 50000--110000 Pa
summary(var_data$psfc[[1]])

# shortwave: 0--1400 W/m2
summary(var_data$swdnb[[1]])







Sanity checks



WRF output is model output, not observations. Temperature below 200 K, negative shortwave, or negative pressure indicate a data problem – don’t pass those through to the model.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Write CF-standard NetCDF
cae_write_cf_netcdf() handles unit conversion and CF metadata in one call:

cae_write_cf_netcdf(
  var_list = var_data,
  outfile = "CESM2.ssp370.2014-09-01.cf.nc",
  dt_seconds = 3600,
  overwrite = TRUE
)

The output includes CF-1.8 global attributes, standard variable names, and computed wind speed. The output is ready for SIPNET ensemble runs.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
Point extraction alternative
For single-site runs, extract time series at a specific location instead of writing the full grid:

pts <- lapply(sipnet_vars, function(v) {
  cae_fetch_point(v, model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                 lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75, n_timesteps = 24)
})
names(pts) <- sipnet_vars




Figure 1: Hourly meteorological variables at Fresno, CA from the CESM2 SSP3-7.0 WRF simulation. These are the inputs SIPNET needs for a single-site carbon flux simulation. The diurnal signatures are physically consistent – peak radiation drives peak temperature with a lag, humidity drops as temperature rises, and pressure follows a semidiurnal tide.

title: Building SIPNET Met Drivers from WRF Projections
text:
References

Rahimi, S. et al. (2024). An overview of the Western United States Dynamically Downscaled Dataset (WUS-D3). Geoscientific Model Development, 17, 2265–2286. doi:10.5194/gmd-17-2265-2024
Dietze, M. C. et al. (2014). A quantitative assessment of a terrestrial biosphere model’s data needs across North American biomes.
